data.title: Skillz receives NYSE notice regarding late form 10-K filing
data.description:
• Skillz (NYSE:SKLZ) said on Tuesday that it has received a notice from the New York Stock Exchange that the company is not in compliance with the NYSE’s continued listing requirements because the company has not timely filed its annual report. 
  • The NYSE Notice has no immediate effect on the listing of the company’s common stock on the NYSE. The company has six months from March 17, 2025, to regain compliance with the NYSE listing standards by filing the Form 10-K with the SEC.
  • Skillz (NYSE:SKLZ) shares were down 0.26% in extended trading. 
  •

data.title: Nike slides to five-year low amid relentless tariff drama
data.description:
Nike (NYSE:NKE) shed 4.3% on Tuesday and carved out a new five-year low of $52.38 earlier in the session. In the latest twist, the Trump administration confirmed that it plans to apply 104% tariffs on China starting at 12:01 a.m. Wednesday. The threat was in response to Beijing upping its tariff rate on U.S. products.

Nike (NYSE:NKE) manufactures approximately 50% of its footwear in Vietnam, with additional production in Indonesia and China. Of note, the new tariffs—46% on Vietnam, 32% on Indonesia, and 54% on China—substantially raise the cost of importing goods into the U.S., which is expected to lead to higher prices for U.S. consumers. At the same time, moving manufacturing to the U.S. would also lead to much higher prices, according to industry insiders.

The athletic apparel stock is down 34% since the tariff headlines starting sweeping in a few weeks ago. The spotlight on Nike's (NKE) manufacturing out of Asia has overshadowed some of the indications that Nike (NKE) was seeing momentum under new CEO Elliot Hill.

Analysts have highlighted that recent footwear releases from Nike (NKE) have seen strong consumer interest, according to search trends and survey results. Hill's strategic direction is also widely seen as being on track.

data.title: Bitcoin resumes slide as U.S. equities waver sharply
data.description:
Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) resilience proved short-lived Tuesday as U.S. equities erased gains from early in the session.

After briefly bouncing to the $80K level, the first-ever crypto (BTC-USD)  fell back to $76.8K, down 2.9% over the last 24 hours, as of 3:56 p.m. ET. It wasn't until the stock market opened that bitcoin began falling with it, underscoring their current strong (albeit sometimes shaky) relationship.

A slew of other major tokens also got hit, with Ether (ETH-USD) down 5.5% to $1.46K, Near (NEAR-USD) -9.9%, Shiba Inu (SHIB-USD) -6.4%, Staked Ether (STETH-USD) -5.7%, Ondo (ONDO-USD) -5.3% and Cronos (CRO-USD) -5.3%.

Crypto-related stocks/ETFs: (IBIT) -1.5%,  (FETH) -4.7%, (MSTR) -11%, (MARA) -8.7%, (RIOT) -9.8%, (OTCPK:BRPHF) +6.3%, (BKKT) -9.6%, (BTBT) -7.8%, (HIVE) -8.5%, (HUT) -4.6%.

The swings coincide with another topsy-turvy trading session on Wall Street, as a U.S. government official doused hopes of any tariff exemptions and a Federal Reserve speaker largely spoke negatively about the trade barriers.

Year-to-date, bitcoin (BTC-USD) shed 19%, while the S&P 500 Index retreated 14%.

data.title: Suburban Propane rated Sell equivalent at Mizuho as clean energy story gets murkier
data.description:
Suburban Propane Partners (NYSE:SPH) -1.4% in Tuesday's trading but still up 11% YTD as Mizuho maintains an Underperform rating and $17 price target despite likely near-term upside in Q2 results from cold January and February weather that should boost propane volumes and contrast with disappointing Q1 results.

Suburban Propane (NYSE:SPH) units have remained resilient despite the announced $100M equity ATM program, which Mizuho's Gabriel Moreen expects will ease balance sheet constraints as leverage ticked up to 5.0x in Q1.

Based on conversations with management and developments across the clean energy space, Moreen anticipates Suburban Propane's (SPH) forthcoming earnings could feature disappointing updates on its renewable natural gas investments regarding monetization of production tax credits and LCFS credit outlook.

The analyst expect the company to take a pause from incremental renewables investment beyond current projects until regulatory clarity emerges.

Suburban Propane (SPH) features relative insulation from tariffs, and could see modest tailwinds from lower propane prices, but with corresponding offsets, Moreen writes.

data.title: Lack of fall in bond yields ‘disconcerting’ with recession risks: BCA’s Berezin
data.description:
U.S. bond yields moving up in the face of recession fears raises the prospect of deeper trouble for the world’s largest economy, BCA Research’s Chief Global Strategist Peter Berezin told Seeking Alpha. 

Long-term Treasury yields (US10Y)(US30Y) rose Tuesday, following big surges in Monday’s wild day for in the fixed-income and equity (SP500)(DJI)(COMP:IND) markets alike. Markets have been rocked as the Trump administration pursues large-scale tariffs on trading partners, stoking recession warnings from economists. 

“One thing that I find rather disconcerting now is that bond yields haven't fallen,” Berezin told Seeking Alpha in a Tuesday interview. “Usually, bond yields fall during periods of growth worries, and that helps to support the economy. We haven't seen that this time around,” he said. 

“That means that if there's a recession, unless those bond yields start going down, [a recession] will probably be deeper than it would be otherwise,” he said. Berezin has a bearish S&P 500 (SP500) target of 4,450 in anticipating a recession stoked by U.S. tariff policy. 

Berezin sees a few forces keeping bond yields higher. 

“One, inflation is going to go up, and so that limits the ability of the Fed to cut rates. Two, foreigners may not see Treasuries as a super-duper safe asset anymore. And three, the U.S. fiscal situation is pretty grim,” he said. 

The strategist said investors may be reluctant to own a substantial amount of Treasuries if the U.S. is heading into a recession, facing lower tax revenue, and spending more on Medicaid and unemployment insurance, leading the deficit to rise.

“On top of that, [President Donald] Trump is promising big, unfunded tax cuts, and if there's a recession, that's going to even further justify the case for fiscal easing,” Berezin said. “So the market's sniffing all of that out and not really pushing down yields the way that normally occurs.” 

The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) climbed 7 basis points to 4.25% on Tuesday, and the 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) increased 9 basis points to 4.71%. 

Investors can get exposure to Treasuries via ETFs such as (NASDAQ:TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), and (BIL).

data.title: Rpm international outlines Q4 sales target with flat sales projection and modest earnings growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: RPM International Inc. (RPM) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Frank Sullivan highlighted adverse weather conditions as a major factor affecting Q3 results, particularly in the Southern and Western U.S., which are typically active during winter. Weather-related headwinds and softness in specialty OEM markets counteracted improvements from MAP 2025 and SG&A streamlining.
  • The company achieved its second-best ever Q3 operating cash flow, driven by disciplined production levels and inventory reductions, though this temporarily pressured margins.
  • New product launches in the Consumer segment included the Mean Green refillable household cleaner and Rust-Oleum low odor aerosol paint. Both products aim to target sustainability and efficiency, with the former recently winning a Better Homes and Gardens award.
  • RPM announced the definitive agreement to acquire The Pink Stuff, a global leader in household cleaning products, to expand their offerings. Calendar 2024 sales for The Pink Stuff were approximately GBP150 million, with RPM aiming to leverage its expertise and operational footprint to drive growth.
  • CFO Rusty Gordon noted that Q4 sales are expected to be flat, with modest earnings growth and margin expansion anticipated, driven by MAP 2025 benefits and easier year-over-year comparisons in some segments.

Outlook

  • The company projects consolidated Q4 sales to remain flat but expects modest earnings growth and margin expansion. Performance Coatings Group is anticipated to lead growth, supported by high-performance building projects, while other segments face mixed conditions.
  • Specialty Products Group is expected to experience low single-digit sales declines due to weak demand in specialty OEM markets, offset partly by market share gains.
  • Management indicated continued focus on MAP 2025 initiatives, which will extend into fiscal 2026, targeting further efficiency improvements.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated sales declined by 3% year-over-year, with unfavorable weather conditions and foreign currency impacts being major contributors.
  • Adjusted EBIT declined by $31.9 million due to lower production volumes, foreign exchange impacts, and temporary inefficiencies from plant consolidations and startups.
  • Consumer Group achieved slight organic growth, supported by new product introductions and market share gains in the cleaners segment, despite inflationary pressures.
  • The company reported $91.5 million in Q3 operating cash flow, marking the second strongest Q3 in its history.

Q&A

  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research Partners: Asked about macro growth assumptions and operating rate impacts. CEO Sullivan responded that the company assumes a low-to-no growth environment and emphasized a focus on outperforming through MAP initiatives and market share gains.
  • Mike Sison, Wells Fargo: Inquired about the company’s resilience in the event of a U.S. recession. CEO Sullivan highlighted RPM's strong repair and maintenance focus and product offerings tailored to economic downturns.
  • Mike Harrison, Seaport Research Partners: Asked about the weather impact on Q3 performance and potential Q4 recovery. CEO Sullivan noted that most delayed projects due to weather are expected to resume in Q4.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns over weather-related disruptions and their impact on sales and operating rates. The tone was cautious, reflecting uncertainty about macroeconomic conditions and potential tariff effects.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing MAP 2025 progress and new growth initiatives. However, in Q&A exchanges, there was a more defensive tone when addressing weather impacts and market challenges.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted slightly to emphasize mitigating external challenges while maintaining a focus on structural improvements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management revised Q4 guidance to flat sales and modest earnings growth, reflecting a more cautious stance compared to prior expectations for stronger growth.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from broader MAP initiative impacts to more immediate concerns, such as weather disruptions and tariff-related cost pressures.
  • Consumer Group experienced slight organic growth, reversing the prior quarter's flat performance, aided by new product launches and market share gains.
  • Sentiment from both management and analysts showed increased caution compared to Q2, with a focus on mitigating external pressures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Weather disruptions significantly affected Q3 performance, delaying outdoor construction activities and impacting sales across key segments.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential impacts from tariffs on raw materials, particularly in areas like resins and packaging. Management outlined mitigation strategies, including alternative sourcing and price adjustments.
  • The economic environment remains a critical factor, with a low-to-no growth outlook influencing RPM's cautious guidance.

Final Takeaway

RPM International Inc. faced significant weather-related challenges in Q3 2025, leading to a decline in sales and profitability compared to the prior year. However, the company emphasized progress through its MAP 2025 initiatives, achieving strong operating cash flow and introducing innovative products in the Consumer segment. Looking ahead, management anticipates a flat sales environment in Q4, with modest earnings growth driven by operational improvements and new product contributions, while focusing on mitigating external risks and leveraging strategic acquisitions like The Pink Stuff to enhance its position in key markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dominion downgraded to Sell equivalent at J.P. Morgan on CVOW tariff troubles
data.description:
Dominion Energy (NYSE:D) -2.9% in Tuesday's trading as J.P. Morgan downgrades shares to Underperform from Neutral with a $52 price target, trimmed from $59, citing concerns about the growing risks associated with the company’s Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project.

JPM analysts led by Jeremy Tonet say the CVOW project still requires a ~$2.5B investment outside of the U.S., and potential costs could rise by an additional $500M-$625M from exposure to steel tariffs and trade levies involving the European Union and Mexico on top of still to-be-finalized PJM network upgrades, which already have seen a $600M cost increase in Dominion's (NYSE:D) last revision.

Dominion's (D) growing project risk on uncontrollable external factors is "inconsistent with qualities desired within the broader group as part of any safety trade, negative against a balance 
sheet requiring regular external equity funding and carrying a high near-term dividend payout, and underpins unfavorable quarterly update optics until construction completion, where each report seemingly carries the potential for higher CVOW costs and incremental shareholder hits," Tonet writes.

The JPM team also upgrades Southern (SO) to Neutral from Underweight as "a calm harbor amidst the storm," and WEC Energy (WEC) to Neutral from Underweight as a "de-risked regulatory outlook [and] constructive Wisconsin backdrop" combine for steadiness.

data.title: SPY ETF short-sellers are looking at mark-to-market YTD profits of nearly $10B
data.description:
Market participants betting against the world's third-largest exchange-traded fund stand to make an estimated $9.6B this year in mark-to-market paper profits, following Wall Street's biggest crisis since the COVID-19 stock market crash in 2020. 

According to data from S3 Partners shared with Seeking Alpha, short-sellers betting against the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY) are looking at mark-to-market paper profits of an estimated $9.6B YTD.

As per S3, $7B of those profits were attributed to last week's historic Thursday-Friday crash that saw the S&P 500 (SP500) crater more than -10%. It was one of the largest two-day declines for the benchmark index in four decades. 

Launched in January 1993, SPY was the very first ETF listed in the U.S. It tracks the S&P (SP500), and has assets under management of $520.55B.

According to the data shared by S3, the short interest in SPY as of April 4 was 118M shares, or 11.6% outstanding. The short interest notional was $59.2B.

Here are some other major ETFs that track the benchmark S&P 500: (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: Apple sinks as Leavitt says Trump believes iPhones can be built in US
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) shares extended their losses and fell 4.5% on Tuesday after White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said President Trump believes iPhones can be built in the U.S.

Trump believes the U.S. has the workforce and resources needed to build the smartphones domestically, Leavitt said at her press briefing. Leavitt's comments came after U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said earlier in the day that Trump would not provide exemptions to his tariffs for individual products or companies.

In the 2018 trade war between the U.S. and China, Trump had granted an exception to Apple for its iPhones, which are largely manufactured out of China. 

Last week, Wedbush Securities analyst Dan Ives said the tariff uncertainty brought forth by President Trump “turns the supply Chain upside down” and could result in iPhones costing as much as $3,500 — a figure some have disputed — up from around $1,000.

“[The] concept of making iPhones in the US is a non-starter in our view at $1,000,” Ives wrote. “Price points would move up so dramatically it's hard to comprehend, and the near-term margin impact on Apple's gross margins during this tariff war could be mind-boggling for this US tech stalwart.”

Despite efforts to diversify its supply chain geographically, Apple still relies on China for a significant portion of its manufacturing needs, specifically iPhones. Investment firm Needham recently estimated that Apple's fiscal 2025 earnings could be negatively impacted by 28% or more in a trade war between China and the U.S. unless it was given an exemption.

Last week, China unveiled a 34% tariff on certain U.S. goods in response to President Trump's announcement that all goods emanating from China (and every other country) would be hit with a tariff amid his concerns about trade deficits. The situation has devolved into a war of words, as Trump said the U.S. would impose additional tariffs on China of 50%, effective April 9th, if its 34% levy was not withdrawn. China subsequently said it will “fight to the end” after Trump threatened an additional tariff.

The Times of India reported that Apple sent five airplanes worth of iPhones from India to the U.S. last week to avoid the 10% tariff charges placed on India. The Wall Street Journal subsequently reported that Apple would seek to move more of its iPhone production to India. This highlights the extreme steps companies are taking in light of Trump's tariff announcement.

Apple releases its second quarter fiscal 2025 financial results on May 1. A consensus estimate expects Apple to report earnings per share of $1.61 on revenue of $94.04B.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
